title: The Six Major Hadith Collections: A Complete Guide to the Kutub al-Sittah
excerpt: Sahih Bukhari, Sahih Muslim, Sunan Abu Dawud, Tirmidhi, Nasa'i, Ibn Majah — these six hadith collections are the foundation of Islamic knowledge. This guide explains each one, their grading systems, and how to use them correctly.
author: QuranBookk
content:
After the Quran, the Hadith — the recorded sayings, actions, and approvals of the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ — is the most important source of Islamic guidance. The Prophet ﷺ himself said:

<HadithBlock
  text="I have left among you two things; you will never go astray as long as you hold to them: the Book of Allah and the Sunnah of His Prophet."
  narrator="Malik ibn Anas (رضي الله عنه)"
  source="Al-Muwatta 1661 — Authenticated as sahih through other chains"
/>

For nearly 200 years after the Prophet's death ﷺ, his sayings were memorised and transmitted orally by chains of trustworthy narrators. The great hadith scholars of the 3rd Islamic century undertook the monumental task of collecting, verifying, and classifying these narrations — producing the collections we rely on today.

## What Is a Hadith?

A hadith has two components:

**Isnad (chain of narrators)** — the sequence of people who transmitted the report, going back to the Prophet ﷺ. For example: "A told me that B told him that C heard from the Prophet ﷺ that he said..."

**Matn (text)** — the actual content of what the Prophet ﷺ said or did.

The science of hadith (*mustalah al-hadith*) developed extraordinarily rigorous methods to verify both the chain and the text — examining the character of every narrator, their memory, whether they could have actually met the people they were claiming to have heard from, and whether the text contradicted more reliable reports. This system of verification is arguably the most sophisticated historical verification methodology ever developed by any civilisation.

## The Grading System

Hadith are classified into several categories:

**Sahih (Authentic)** — the chain is unbroken, every narrator is trustworthy and precise, and the text does not contradict anything more reliable. This is the highest grade.

**Hasan (Good)** — slightly weaker narrator chain than sahih but still reliable and acted upon.

**Da'if (Weak)** — a defect exists in the chain or narrator reliability. Not acted upon in matters of belief or law, though some scholars use them for virtuous deeds (fada'il al-a'mal) with conditions.

**Mawdu' (Fabricated)** — falsely attributed to the Prophet ﷺ. To narrate a fabricated hadith while knowing it is fabricated is forbidden.

## The Kutub al-Sittah: The Six Canonical Collections

### 1. Sahih al-Bukhari

**Compiler**: Muhammad ibn Ismail al-Bukhari (194–256 AH / 810–870 CE)
**Hadiths**: 7,563 (with repetitions); approximately 2,602 without
**Status**: Universally regarded as the most authentic book after the Quran

Al-Bukhari reportedly examined 600,000 narrations and selected only those meeting his rigorous standard. He is said to have performed ghusl and prayed two rak'ahs before recording each hadith. The collection is organised by chapters of fiqh (Islamic law), making it both a hadith collection and a legal reference.

**Distinctive feature**: Al-Bukhari's conditions for accepting a hadith were the strictest of all collectors — requiring that two narrators in the chain must have actually met, not merely lived at the same time.
**Best for**: Beginning students of hadith, accessing the most reliably authenticated reports.

👉 [Read Sahih al-Bukhari on QuranBookk](/hadiths/hadiths-sources/bukhari)

### 2. Sahih Muslim

**Compiler**: Muslim ibn al-Hajjaj al-Naysaburi (204–261 AH / 820–875 CE)
**Hadiths**: 7,500 (with repetitions); approximately 3,033 without
**Status**: The second most authentic book after the Quran, alongside Sahih al-Bukhari

Muslim studied under al-Bukhari and shared similar standards. His collection is organised differently — all narrations of a single topic are grouped together, making it easier to see all the chains for one report in one place. This layout makes Sahih Muslim particularly useful for hadith scholarship.

**Distinctive feature**: Muslim's introduction to his Sahih is itself a masterwork of hadith methodology (mustalah).
**Best for**: Advanced students who want to compare multiple chains for the same report.

### 3. Sunan Abu Dawud

**Compiler**: Abu Dawud Sulayman ibn al-Ash'ath (202–275 AH / 817–889 CE)
**Hadiths**: 5,274
**Status**: Primary reference for Islamic jurisprudence (fiqh)

Abu Dawud explicitly focused his collection on hadiths with legal implications. He reportedly examined 500,000 narrations and selected approximately 4,800 of legal relevance. Unlike the two Sahihs, Sunan Abu Dawud includes some weak hadiths — but Abu Dawud helpfully notes which are weak and why.

**Distinctive feature**: The foremost hadith collection for fiqh rulings. Every chapter corresponds to a legal question.
**Best for**: Understanding Islamic law and practice.

### 4. Jami' al-Tirmidhi

**Compiler**: Muhammad ibn Isa al-Tirmidhi (209–279 AH / 824–892 CE)
**Hadiths**: 3,956
**Status**: Highly valued for its gradings and jurisprudential notes

Al-Tirmidhi is unique among the six because he notes the legal school that acted upon each hadith, mentions variant narrations, and provides his own grading for each report. For a student trying to understand *how* hadith relates to fiqh across the different madhabs, Tirmidhi is invaluable.

**Distinctive feature**: Contains the grades *hasan sahih*, *hasan gharib*, and others that al-Tirmidhi developed — expanding the grading vocabulary of the science.
**Best for**: Connecting hadith to fiqh differences between schools.

### 5. Sunan al-Nasa'i

**Compiler**: Ahmad ibn Shu'ayb al-Nasa'i (215–303 AH / 829–915 CE)
**Hadiths**: 5,761 (al-Mujtaba); 11,000+ (the larger al-Sunan al-Kubra)
**Status**: Known for stringent criticism of narrators

Al-Nasa'i was so strict in his narrators that he accepted fewer hadiths than even al-Bukhari on some topics. He was particularly expert in identifying hidden defects (*'ilal*) in hadith chains that others missed.

**Distinctive feature**: The most stringent narrator criticism of the six collections after the two Sahihs.

### 6. Sunan Ibn Majah

**Compiler**: Muhammad ibn Yazid ibn Majah (209–273 AH / 824–887 CE)
**Hadiths**: 4,341
**Status**: The sixth of the Kutub al-Sittah, though scholars differed on including it

Ibn Majah's collection contains approximately 600 hadiths found nowhere else in the six collections — a unique contribution. However, it also contains more weak and problematic narrations than the other five, requiring more scholarly caution.

**Best for**: Finding comprehensive coverage of topics, used alongside the other collections.

## The Earlier Collection: Al-Muwatta of Imam Malik

Though not formally part of the Kutub al-Sittah, Imam Malik's **Al-Muwatta** (compiled around 159 AH) is the oldest surviving hadith collection and one of the most respected. Al-Shafi'i said: *"There is no book on earth after the Quran that is more correct than the book of Malik."* (Pre-Bukhari era — later scholars revised this ranking with Sahih al-Bukhari at the top.)

## How to Study Hadith Correctly

<Callout type="warning">
Never apply a hadith to legal rulings without qualified scholarly guidance. Hadith misapplication — especially of weak or context-dependent reports — is a significant source of confusion in the Muslim community. Read hadith to increase knowledge and love of the Prophet ﷺ; for legal application, consult a qualified scholar.
</Callout>

**Start with the two Sahihs** — begin with the most reliably graded collections.
**Read with commentary** — works like *Fath al-Bari* (Ibn Hajar's commentary on Bukhari) and *Sharh al-Nawawi* (Nawawi's commentary on Muslim) provide the scholarly context essential to understanding each hadith correctly.
**Learn basic hadith terminology** — understanding terms like *isnad*, *matn*, *mursal*, *munqati'*, *da'if*, *mawdu'* prevents misapplication.

## Explore All Hadith Collections on QuranBookk

QuranBookk provides the complete Kutub al-Sittah in a clean, searchable format — with Arabic text, English translations, and grading information. Browse by collection, chapter, or keyword.

👉 [Explore all authentic hadith collections on QuranBookk](/hadiths/hadiths-sources)

The hadith are the living legacy of the Prophet ﷺ — his words, his silences, his laughter, his grief, his generosity, and his worship. To know the hadith is to know the Prophet ﷺ, and to know him is the greatest intellectual and spiritual pursuit available to a Muslim.
